Sustainable development strategies for Smart Cities: Review and development framework
=====================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Jiangjiang Shao a, Byunghak Min b

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cities.2024.105663](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cities.2024.105663 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0264275124008771&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •Bibliometric analysis for quickly locating important and pertinent literature 
*   •Review the recent advances in the research and development of smart cities 
*   •Synthesize the understanding of smart sustainable cities 
*   •Establish a framework for smart sustainable cities 

Abstract
--------

The emergence of new technologies, such as information and communication technologies and the Internet of Things, is making cities more digital and intelligent as we move into the fourth industrial revolution. The smart sustainable cities (SSCs) concept is human-centered, involving the multidimensional integration of people and digitization, predicting the future development of global cities. However, the concept might not apply to all cities around the world. In this paper, a quantitative bibliometric analysis of 4643 published articles on smart cities (SCs) and other related research is conducted, and aims to develop a framework with multiple dimensions to help improve the understanding of the concept applicability. The main finding reveals that four pillars, namely environment, society, governance, and economy, are integrated into the framework to improve the understanding of the concept capability. This framework can allow urban planners and policymakers to assess the effectiveness of the concept in their practice projects.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008710)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008680)

Keywords
--------

Smart city

Bibliometric analysis

Sustainable development

Smart sustainable cities framework

1. Introduction
---------------

According to the United Nations' Worldwide Urbanization Prospects report ([UN, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0795)), >4.3 billion people worldwide presently reside in urban areas, and 68% of the world's population is expected to live in cities by 2050. The quality of life (QoL) for urban dwellers and the sustainable operation and management of cities have faced significant challenges due to urbanization and the increase in the permanent urban population. To this end, the World Commission on Environment and Development originally defined sustainable development (SD) in 1987 in terms of its economic, social, and environmental aspects ([Redclift, 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0645)).

Schwab first used the term “Fourth Industrial Revolution” (4IR) at the World Economic Forum (WEF) in 2016 ([Schwab, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0695)). As technology advances, it drastically alters how we live and work in the modern world by spurring constant technological innovation and accelerating the pace of technological change ([Hyun Park et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0405); [Ross & Maynard, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0655)). For instance, the advent of early digital cities (DCs) ([Ishida, 2002](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0415)) and smart cities (SCs) ([Batty et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0085)), as well as many other technological advancements, has presented opportunities and challenges for urban research and practice ([Ying et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0900)).

Driven by new technologies, the efficient management of information and resources, the effective safeguarding of citizen welfare, and the sustainability of urban spaces have presented significant challenges for the development of smart cities (SCs) ([Javed et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0420)). Research on SCs has expanded from technology-driven approaches to include public engagement and innovative social governance ([Cowley et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0220); [Luo & Huang, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0530)). Broadly speaking, SD is the goal of urban development, while SCs are the technological means to achieve this goal ([Bibri, 2018a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0090)). The main controversy over whether SCs are sustainable or not lies in the confusion between the ends and means of urban development ([Su & Fan, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0750)). Therefore, there is a need for more research to distinguish the role of SD, SCs and Smart Sustainable cities (SSCs).

In the existing studies, which mostly focus on empirical studies assessing the different content of SDGs and the description and establishment of indicators ([Sharifi et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0720)), there is a lack of a concise framework for sustainability of SC strategies for most different cities. Although some studies have pointed out that the sustainability of SCs is closely related in the sustainable triple bottom line and the dimensions of the ESG (Environmental, Social, and Governance) frameworks ([Eskantar et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0300); [Ribeiro et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0650)). And they have been studied as a framework for SC sustainability. However, there is a lack of evidence that its dimensions are directly related in SSCs ([Sharifi et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0725)). As well as, how to highlight the local differentiation in the face of the process of constructing SSCs in different cities. So, the aim is to develop a framework with multiple dimensions to help improve the understanding of the concept applicability.

2. Literature review
--------------------

This section presents a theoretical account of key concepts SCs, SSCs and applicational limitation with SSCs.

### 2.1. Smart Cities

Information and communication technologies (ICTs) have become ingrained in every aspect of social life in the context of globalization and the information age, and they have greatly influenced urban development ([Roy, 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0665); [Wang et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0830)). The Internet of Things (IOTs), along with ICTs and other new technologies, are being used in urban research, with the primary goal of creating SCs through the integration of new and existing technologies to address urban issues ([Rani et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0635)). The current body of research on SCs is closely related to the implementation of SCs and has progressively focused on developing further levels of research, such as technological innovation and social ethics, from the initial theoretical exploration ([Bibri, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0105); [Javed et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0425); [Mora et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0575)). As such, the theoretical and technical aspects of SCs have received a great deal of attention from current political and academic circles, and the study of SCs has grown in popularity in the field of urban research ([Gupta et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0365)).

Based on the maturity of the new technology, therefore the term “smart city”, as the focus of an area of study in urban science, has its roots in the late 1990s smart growth ([Harrison & Donnelly, 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0370)). Compared to other traditional industries, big data has a greater influence on urban planning and urban research due to its rapid development as a research field and its development direction in ICTs ([Thakuriah et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0760)). The term “smart city” was first used by IBM in 2010 ([Dirks & Keeling, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0260)), and the definition of an SC varies depending on the urban development strategies, policy and practice of various nations. For example, scholars such as [Joss et al. (2019)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0440) mention in the SCs global discourse network that the sequential subordination of different strategic dimensions in different countries leads to differences in the practice of smart cities. However, emerging ICTs are used to spur creativity in urban planning, bringing intelligence to the environments in which people live and work, with a primary goal of enhancing the well-being, efficiency, and QoL of inhabitants ([Höjer & Wangel, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0380); [Shamsuzzoha et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0715)).

SCs were originally defined primarily in terms of technology ([Jorna & Veenstra, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0435)). The development of SCs is largely driven by the extensive application of ICTs and is continually enhanced through the practices adopted by various countries ([Neirotti et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0585)). However, the definition of a “smart city” is more nuanced and humane than merely technological ([Camero & Alba, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0150)). As theories advance, new technologies become more widely used, new research methods are proposed, and definitions are continuously improved. To account for technological, socio-humanitarian, and operational management dimensions, SC initiatives are typically classified to serve the following six aspects: economy, environment, governance, living, mobility, and the people ([Caragliu et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0165)). Because of the inherent complexity in the implementation and operation of SCs across various dimensions, Al Sharif and other researchers have also conducted corresponding studies to identify and evaluate these six dimensions ([Al Sharif & Pokharel, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0020)). To date, numerous strategies have been developed to increase the level of intelligence of cities ([Broccardo et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0135); [Meijer & Bolívar, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0545); [Paskaleva, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0610)).

### 2.2. Smart sustainable cities

From a technological perspective, SCs are clearly innovative in the field of urban research and development, and most scholarly attention is directed toward these developments and the introduction of new technologies. Innovation is viewed as a tool to help city managers integrate urban policies and measures with sustainability ([Tura & Ojanen, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0790)).

Nevertheless, the SC concept is not only about establishing new technologies as ends in themselves but rather about establishing technologies that contribute to the SD of SCs ([Ahvenniemi et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0015); [Keshavarzi et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0455)). The SC concept has also shifted from a traditional technology focus to a people focus ([Schiavo & Magalhães, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0685)). As a result, reviewing and controlling the results of SC initiatives are necessary. Numerous academics have conducted research on sustainability assessment techniques for SCs ([Girardi & Temporelli, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0345); [Valencia-Arias et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0800)).

Utilizing ICTs and other related technologies to address sustainability issues ([van den Buuse & Kolk, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0805)), boosting the operational effectiveness of standard cities, and enhancing the quality of service (QoS) provided to city dwellers are the fundamental objectives of SCs ([Silva et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0740)). [Harrison et al. (2010)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0375) emphasized the integration of social, commercial, and IT infrastructures in SCs and their use of collective urban intelligence. [Nam and Pardo (2011)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0580) noted that SCs can utilize physical information infrastructure (sensors, IOTs, etc.) to support energy-efficient resource governance and facilitate cross-domain cooperation through data sharing ([Bibri, 2018b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0095)). [Trombin et al. (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0785) highlighted the importance of combining social and human capital with traditional transportation and ICT facilities for governance. [Antrobus (2011)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0055) argued that SCs should prioritize a low-carbon economy and be viewed through the lens of eco-energy. Additionally, [Bakıcı et al. (2013)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0070) stressed the need to consider a broader range of SC perspectives, including sustainability and QoL. Even SSCs are also emerging as a new paradigm for municipal cooperation in some policies and practices ([Yeh, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0885)).

The SC concept has become one of the proposed solutions to the sustainability problems faced by cities in the process of rapid urbanization ([Toli & Murtagh, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0770)). The promotion of SSCs research has become a globalized phenomenon. Currently, the practice of developing an SC has proven successful in the promotion of urban sustainability and innovation ([Bibri & Krogstie, 2017b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0115); [Evans et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0310)). SCs have different policies and technological support in various countries and regions, while SD is supported by global policies. The integration of SCs and SD is a necessity for the future of cities ([Schraven et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0690)).

### 2.3. The applicational limitation with SSCs

Initially, scholars such as Yigitcanlar also questioned the sustainability of SCs. In his study, where the UK was used as an example, a positive correlation between the adoption of technology and sustainable outcomes was not found ([Yigitcanlar & Kamruzzaman, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0890)). In addition, the input construction of SCs in [Macke et al.'s (2018)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0535) study was technically complex and did not give positive feedback to residents. The massive investment in smart city construction in Songdo, South Korea also went into decline ([Huh et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0395)). In [Sengupta and Sengupta's (2022)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0700) study, government-led SC construction was similarly at risk of program failure. Dominant approaches likewise affect the sustainability of SCs ([Barr et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0080)). Failures and disagreements in these cases emphasize the importance of balance between technical, social, and economic factors ([Bonab et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0120); [Esmaeilian et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0305)). However, for planners and policymakers unfamiliar with the smart city concept, there is a need for an in-depth explanation of the current state of smart city development, trends, and approaches to smart city building in the context of rapidly evolving technological fields ([Kummitha & Crutzen, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0495); [Neirotti et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0585)).

Assessment methods for assessing the sustainability of SCs vary ([Agbali et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0010); [Huovila et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0400); [Wey & Peng, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0850)), depending on the definition of a SC, or the area in which performance indicators are introduced ([Angelakoglou et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0045); [Girardi & Temporelli, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0345)). Whereas among globally recognized assessment methods, such as CASBEE-UD adopted by the Japanese government ([Sev, 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0705)); BREEAM in the United Kingdom and LEED-ND in the United States, among others ([Kaur & Garg, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0450)) there is a lack of correspondence between them, in particular the absence of indicators for the economic, governance and quality of life dimensions ([Sharifi et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0725)).

Recently, scholars and managers have explored organizational sustainability through the ESG pillars ([Clément et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0200); [Lokuwaduge & Heenetigala, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0525)). Various dependencies and interactions exist among the 17 SDGs indicators and ESG dimensions ([Huang et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0390)). It is evident that in recent decades, there has been a global emphasis on city-level SD research. And supported by global policies of the international community to enable social systems to achieve the objectives of avoiding environmental harm and damage to ecosystems ([Schraven et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0690)). ESG framework widely practiced since 2004 in developed countries like Europe and the US ([Li et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0505)). This framework is extensively used in analyzing enterprise sustainability factors ([Bansal, 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0075); [Delgado-Ceballos et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0250)) and has become a global consensus for corporate development ([Ensign, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0295)). The ‘metabolism’ of cities involves inputs of goods and outputs of waste, extending to social and economic dimensions ([Albino et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0025)). In the case of cities, which are organized in different dimensions, SD is not only social, economic and governance, but also in the areas of circular economy, tourist, educational inputs and urban safety ([de Bem Machado et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0235)). Overall, a comprehensive manner on understanding the SSCs concept with broad dimensions is not widely applied, which may lead to the concept applicable limitations.

This paper aims to develop a framework with multiple dimensions that helps improve understanding of the SSCs concept capability in different cities. To achieve this aim, the following research questions are proposed: What dimensions are included in the developed framework?

The rest of the research content is as follows: In [Section 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#s0030), the research design is described; in [Section 4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#s0050), shows the bibliometric analysis; in [Section 5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#s0095), the results and analysis of thematic content clustering; in [Section 6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#s0120), the developed framework is displayed; in [Section 7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#s0125), discussion and offers a suggested outlook for further research; Finally, [Section 8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#s0130) concludes the paper.

3. Research design
------------------

### 3.1. Step 1. (Data source and search strategy)

The data used in this study were obtained from the core database of the WoS (Web of Science Core Collection). For scientometric analysis, the WoS database was utilized as a suitable data source, thus ensuring authoritative data selection ([Archambault et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0065); [Yao et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275124008771#bb0880)). The search was performed through the databases of articles included in the WoS Core Collection (SCI-EXPANDED, SSCI, AHCI, CPCI-S, CPCI-SSH, and ESCI).The search strategy used keywords as follows: “smart city” (All Fields), or “sustainable development” (All Fields). The time span was set from 01 January 2010 to 31 December 2022 (_T_=2010–2022), and a total of 378,103 articles (_n_=378,103) were retrieved. The language of the article was set to English, the document types were “article” or “review article,” and the research areas were “Urban Studies,” “Architecture” or “Area Studies.” A total of 4871 articles were retrieved (_n_=4871), including 4699 art

FULL CONTENT:
Strategies for Sustainable Urban Planning 7 Peter C althorpe Principal & C o-Founder The foundation of our future rests at the intersection of environmental responsibility, social equity, and economic strength in our cities. Building community consensus is critical to successful urban planning, yet the effort is often fraught with challenges. Particularly in today?
s political climate, the road to widespread adoption and prioritization of city sustainability is dotted with speed bumps. U rban planners are on the frontlines trying to solve these issues, yet they?
ve traditionally faced an uphill battle, armed with dated tools, analytics, and disorganized data. U rbanFootprint was built to bring planners, designers, architects, developers, environmental advocates and analysts together to overcome these obstacles and support a more efficient, sustainable urban planning process. This white paper discusses the seven strategies needed to help turn this vision into reality. As our global population scales to what could one day reach 10 billion people, a new vision of community, prosperity, and urbanism is long overdue. Let?
s work together to help our cities survive and thrive with sustainable design. S incerely, To my fellow urbanists, Peter C althorpe Principal & C o-Founder, C althorpe Analytics UrbanFootprint | 7 Strategies for Sustainable Urban Planning They are more and more self-adjusting, homeostatic and autonomous. In this framework U rbanFootprint changes the way cities can think about themselves. It enables self-awareness (dynamic data), articulates expectations and goals (scenarios), allows reflection (analysis of outcomes), and generates insights (patterns of co-benefits). Practitioners need to steer this process with seven key strategies for building smart, sustainable cities.
The Internet of Things and Big Data are making cities in many ways conscious.
UrbanFootprint | 7 Strategies for Sustainable Urban Planning M ake Connect ions Connect data, people, aspirations, needs, opportunities, futures, and outcom es.
1 UrbanFootprint | 7 Strategies for Sustainable Urban Planning C reating connections is fundamentally what cities do, and it is what designers must do if they are to succeed. G reat cities create connections between people of all ages, incomes, and races. They enhance connections to the natural environment, to history, to local culture, and to goods and services. They are also places that connect ideas, that cross-fertilize, that foster innovation and mix the foreign with the local, the regional with the neighborhood. They are the armature of the global world. More and more the act of creating an urban design, a plan, framing the right infrastructure or public policy, involves enhancing connections. These connections are most typically between stakeholders and special interests that too often see their goals as isolated, view outcomes as winner-take-all, and understand plans as fixed. In addition, we need connections between isolated and growing data sets, stovepipe professional analysis, and facts that too often evade the discussion. C onnecting information in ways that create insight is fundamental to understanding and design.
U rbanFootprint enhances the ability to realize this range of connections; between people, between people and place, between sets of information, and between solutions that reinforce each other. More and more the act of creating an urban design, a plan, framing the right infrastructure or public policy, involves enhancing connections. UrbanFootprint | 7 Strategies for Sustainable Urban Planning Collage Dat a Integrate data to reveal patterns, educate stakeholders, and extend boundaries.
2 UrbanFootprint | 7 Strategies for Sustainable Urban Planning We are swimming in data and it is growing at phenomenal rates.The challenge is to translate this onslaught into useful information. W e are swimming in data and it is growing at phenomenal rates.The challenge is to translate this onslaught into useful information. This involves synthesizing what are now isolated streams and distilling the data into useful information. And this information must be translated into insight and insights must be shared. D igesting data is an interactive practice that involves much more than downloading bits. It involves layering data in ways that reveal new connections, synergies and causalities ? effectively reveling deeper patterns born of isolated data sets. It involves the ability to edit and layer diverse and complex information in ways that enhance understanding a place and setting directions for the future. It also involves asking the right questions easily in ways that integrates many dimensions of input. Just as a collage brings a larger order into focus from fractured pieces, U rbanFootprint allows urban designers to combine, query, and connect data in ways that creates insight and supports solutions. UrbanFootprint | 7 Strategies for Sustainable Urban Planning Solve f or t he Fut ure Design for m ultiple futures rather than perfecting the present.
3 UrbanFootprint | 7 Strategies for Sustainable Urban Planning Much Smart C ities efforts are rightly focused on improving how we, piece by piece, make existing systems more efficient. Software and technology that makes better use of our buildings, roads, services, and open space is critical to setting the stage for a more inclusive and less demanding city. But also needed are tools and practices that shape a more coherent long-range future, that look to the large changes in context and infrastructure that incremental fixes tend to miss. Future land-use patterns and circulation systems are the foundation of better cities, shaping the next generation of investments, systems and policies. W e cannot understand the efficacy of new strategies and infrastructure without setting them in a future framework. U rbanFootprint studies different urban futures, tests new policies, and understands their impacts over time. R esilience, climate change, affordability, and economic growth are dependent on the right combinations of urban form, technology, economic investments, and social norms. W hat is viable today will be obsolete tomorrow if we do not solve for the future. We cannot understand the efficacy of new strategies and infrastructure without setting them in a future framework.
UrbanFootprint | 7 Strategies for Sustainable Urban Planning Build Scenarios Test varied strategies, press extrem es, and find the sw eet spot.
4 UrbanFootprint | 7 Strategies for Sustainable Urban Planning Scenario planning assumes that we cannot predict the future because of unforeseen events and multiple forces. S cenario planning has been used by the private sector and the military for decades for strategic planning based on a range of assumptions. Scenario planning assumes that we cannot predict the future because of unforeseen events and multiple forces. Instead it is a methodology that learns by testing different futures from a matrix of probable primary drivers. It brackets the future and revels strategies that can adapt to differing futures or work with the largest range of outcomes. But there is another reason for building urban scenarios ? to be inclusive of and to test differing preferences of participants. E ach stakeholder and special interest group brings their own ideal future born of their own priorities. These special interest scenarios tend to be shaped around one preferred outcome rather than multiple advantages. Scenario planning allows all to see their idea affirmed as a potential future, analyzed across differing metrics, and then rationally compared to others. In so doing, scenario planning often revels co-benefits and new coalitions between historically isolated interests. This is a catalyst for political change that in turn is the foundation of real change. U rbanFootprint allows planners to build scenarios and run analysis quickly so that sensitivity studies or public preconceptions can be clarified in real time. It also revels the potential co-benefits that can enhance new coalitions and political initiatives. UrbanFootprint | 7 Strategies for Sustainable Urban Planning Seek Co- Benef it s Transform single-issue advocates into coalitions, reveal interconnected outcom es. 5 UrbanFootprint | 7 Strategies for Sustainable Urban Planning Shaping the future of the city depends on consensus and coalitions; it is essentially a political act. C urrent practice typically involves elected officials adjudicating the interests of multiple stakeholders; neighborhood groups, developers, unions, environmentalists, and social equity advocates to name a few. This diverse group of voices often leads to stalemate, delay, and least common denominator outcomes. U ncovering what we call ?
co-benefits? can uncover unseen common interests and coalitions. C omprehensive analysis across a full range of metrics can reveal win-win strategies that demonstrate there are choices that do not involve painful trade-offs. Some virtuous development patterns can solve many problems simultaneously, amplifying cost effectiveness and smoothing the political process. U rbanFootprint can help to find and highlight such co-benefits. Positive social, environmental, and economic outcomes can converge and when they do, political progress is always enhanced. Shaping the future of the city depends on consensus and coalitions; it is essentially a political act. UrbanFootprint | 7 Strategies for Sustainable Urban Planning Be a Generalist Analyze and integrate m ultiple m etrics to end stovepipe thinking.
6 UrbanFootprint | 7 Strategies for Sustainable Urban Planning Planners and designers are inherently generalists; they must integrate across issues, disciplines, and interest groups. Planners and designers are inherently generalists; they must integrate across issues, disciplines, and interest groups. U rban design amplifies this trait, calling on practitioners to understand the social, economic, and environmental dimensions of the built environment. They must balance all of these forces and find ways to communicate whole systems to an audience of specialists. They are conductor and composer of a complex orchestra. O ur cities are too often the product of specialization. W hether developer, engineer, or community group, each actor optimizes for their goals and interests without an overview of systemic impacts, tradeoffs, or synergies ? without a sense of the whole story and its outcome. The role of the generalist, the urbanist in fact, is to understand all the forces at work and find the shared pattern that shapes the narrative. Multi-disciplinary action, though rarely taught, is the practice that creates value through synthesis rather than optimization. U rbanFootprint is intrinsically a tool for G eneralists. It allows planning and design practices to bring together a large range of options and analysis without convening a large team of specialists. It provides a framework for all the special interests to collaborate in an environment that is fact based and professionally sophisticated. UrbanFootprint | 7 Strategies for Sustainable Urban Planning Think Beyond Horizons Stretch boundaries, expand tim efram es, and connect the dots.
7 UrbanFootprint | 7 Strategies for Sustainable Urban Planning D esigners, planners, developers and architects are trained to look beyond the boundaries of their project or site. The larger environment, history, culture, and economies shape the urban landscape whether for small sites, neighborhoods, districts, towns or cities. The metropolitan region is now the platform from which cities interact with the globe. As a result, every project must push beyond static jurisdictional boundaries or simple property lines to the regional context. E xpanding the domain always leads to more sustainable and powerful schemes. It is essential for planning in the 21st century. E xpanding time frames as well as boundaries also enhances the intrinsic value of design. Short term, local thinking too often dominates the debate around significant developments, infrastructure, and policy initiatives -- it is baked into the political process and is hard to overcome. W ith long term visions, life cycle economics, generational social consequences, and geological environmental impacts can come into focus as a part of our public process. U rbanFootprint supports expanding horizons by putting local, regional, and national data at the fingertips of all the stakeholders. It allows practitioners to telescope between scales quickly and efficiently. In addition, U rbanFootprint provides a platform for long-term thinking by facilitating scenarios of differing time frames. The larger environment, history, culture, and economies shape the urban landscape whether for small sites, neighborhoods, districts, towns or cities. UrbanFootprint | 7 Strategies for Sustainable Urban Planning Fundamentally, the way we shape cities is a reflection of the kind of humanity we bring to bear. Urban planners and designers, together with the support of environmental policymakers, analysts, NGOs and advocates, will need to lead the way in building smart, sustainable communities. If we are to unlock political stalemate and address the urgent need to solve for city resiliency and curtail the impacts of climate change, nothing short of a complete transformation of the current urban planning process and supporting technology will do. Today?
s practitioners must dedicate themselves to the practice of building mutually beneficial communities. Let's build a better future with smart city planning.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Effective urban planning strategies integrate environmental, social, and economic dimensions to create sustainable cities that adapt to changing demographics and climate challenges.
Resilient urban frameworks prioritize flexibility, enabling cities to respond to unexpected challenges, such as climate change impacts, while maintaining essential services and quality of life.

Robust monitoring systems are essential for assessing urban policies, ensuring continuous improvement through data-driven insights, which support sustainable urban development and informed decision-making.
